query: stoppage of venlafaxine causes anxiety and insomnia

retrieved_docs.title: Suicide: Assessment and Management -- History and Physical -- Clinical Vignette 1
retrieved_docs.content: Sheila is recently postmenopausal and has a history of hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine. She reports that she had worsening hot flashes that interfere with her sleep. Six weeks ago, her gynecologist tapered her escitalopram from 20 mg daily to 10 mg daily and started venlafaxine extended release at 37.5 mg daily. Two weeks ago, escitalopram was discontinued, and venlafaxine was increased to 75 mg daily. She reports taking this medication as prescribed. Despite a marked improvement in hot flashes, she continued to have difficulty sleeping. Other symptoms include heightened irritability, anhedonia, increased appetite, poor concentration, and decreased energy. She finds meaning in her work and family, though she notes “fleeting” thoughts of “what’s the point of going on?” Sheila denied current thoughts of suicide, any recent or present suicide plan or intention, or any suicide preparations. She feels guilty about feeling sad when her family and colleagues are so supportive of her. She reports taking a few sick days from work due to fatigue, but has not had any significant problems at work. Her affect is constricted. There is no psychomotor retardation. Sheila denies auditory or visual hallucinations and does not appear paranoid or delusional.

retrieved_docs.title: Venlafaxine -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Venlafaxine is prescribed for the long term, and it carries severe risks if not taken as directed or stopped abruptly. Abrupt cessation of venlafaxine can lead to severe adverse effects such as irritability, tiredness, restlessness, anxiety, insomnia, trouble sleeping, nightmares, headache, sweating, dizziness, tingling, or "pins and needles" feeling, shaking, confusion, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. Some patients find withdrawal symptoms uncomfortable. [19] Common adverse effects of venlafaxine include headache, nausea, insomnia, dizziness, somnolence, xerostomia, asthenia, hypertension, impotence, decreased libido, anorgasmia, constipation, weight loss, abnormal dreams, diarrhea, abdominal pain, blurred vision, anxiety, tremor, hypercholesterolemia, and hyponatremia. Severe adverse effects include serotonin syndrome [20] and seizures. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Mood Disorder -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: SSRIs are the first-line treatment option for depressive disorder, as they are tolerated better with lesser side effects. They normally take 4 to 5 weeks to display full effects. When on SSRI, it is important to monitor for suicidal thoughts, especially in young adults. The commonly seen adverse effects are nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dizziness, loss of appetite, reduced sexual desire, anxiety, and insomnia. Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRI) : venlafaxine, desvenlafaxine, duloxetine, milnacipran, and levomilnacipran

retrieved_docs.content: Discontinuation of medications that may exacerbate confusion, memory, and insomnia is essential.

retrieved_docs.title: Venlafaxine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the appropriate indications for venlafaxine therapy in the management of various psychiatric conditions based on the patient's psychiatric diagnosis and symptomatology. Screen patients for contraindications to venlafaxine therapy, such as hypersensitivity reactions or concurrent use of monoamine oxidase inhibitors. Implement appropriate dosing strategies for venlafaxine initiation, titration, and discontinuation based on patient-specific factors and treatment response. Communicate with other healthcare providers to coordinate comprehensive care for patients receiving venlafaxine, including monitoring vital signs and laboratory parameters. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Venlafaxine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Venlafaxine is categorized as a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI). It is approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to treat major depressive disorder (unipolar), social anxiety disorder, and panic disorder. In addition, venlafaxine is commonly used off-label for conditions such as attention-deficit disorder, fibromyalgia, diabetic neuropathy, complex pain syndromes, hot flashes, migraine prevention, posttraumatic stress disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and premenstrual dysphoric disorder. Venlafaxine can be used either as a standalone treatment or as part of a combination therapy regimen with other medications.

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Although not considered a withdrawal syndrome, abrupt discontinuation of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) can lead to distressing symptoms termed SSRI drug discontinuation syndrome. Symptoms include gastrointestinal distress, dizziness, sleep disturbance, anxiety, irritability, and poor concentration. These symptoms develop between 1 to 7 days after discontinuation of the SSRI, after being on the medication for at least 30 days. This is not believed to be a remanifestation of the original disorder. Treatment included reinstituting the medication with a slow taper if discontinuation is to be achieved. [77] [78] [79]

retrieved_docs.title: Venlafaxine -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Interprofessional healthcare team members should monitor vital signs, particularly blood pressure, during venlafaxine therapy. Additionally, venlafaxine patients should undergo regular assessments of renal function and lipid profiles. Close monitoring of psychiatric symptoms, including suicidality, depression, mania, anxiety, or unusual behavior changes, is imperative. Careful attention should be exercised during transitions between venlafaxine and MAOIs, with a recommended 2-week gap before starting venlafaxine after discontinuing an MAOI and a 7-day gap before commencing MAOI therapy after stopping venlafaxine. Collaboration among clinicians and pharmacists is essential to facilitate comprehensive monitoring, ensure patient safety, and optimize therapeutic outcomes. This approach underscores the significance of evidence-based medicine in guiding psychiatric clinical practice while tailoring care to meet individual patient needs.

retrieved_docs.title: Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder -- Treatment / Management -- 2. Pharmacological Methods
retrieved_docs.content: Psychotropic agents Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SRIs): SRIs have been proven to be effective in the treatment of severe mood and somatic symptoms of PMDD. The ones that have been particularly linked with the relief of symptoms are Clomipramine (a tricyclic antidepressant), [41] [42] [41] Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors like citalopram, [43] [44] escitalopram, fluoxetine, paroxetine and sertraline, and noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor venlafaxine. Antidepressants that predominantly affect noradrenergic transmission are not as effective for PMDD as SRIs, which means that the effect of SRIs in PMDD is not just an antidepressant effect. [45] [46] This is supported by the fact that the beneficial effect of SRIs begins rapidly in PMDD, whereas antidepressant effect takes several weeks. Thus, clinicians can use SRIs intermittently from mid-cycle to menses to treat symptoms of PMDD as opposed to continuous treatment [47] . Side effects of SRIs are usually mild. Nausea is the most common adverse effect, but it usually wears off in a couple of days after starting the therapy and doesn’t reappear even if the therapy is intermittent. Reduced libido and anorgasmia are other common adverse effects, but they are absent in drug-free intervals. Benzodiazepines (BZDs): BZDs like alprazolam have been found to be effective only in women with severe anxiety and premenstrual insomnia. However, since there is a risk of dependence, careful monitoring is required, especially in cases with reported prior substance abuse. [48] [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Generalized Anxiety Disorder -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with anxiety disorders need to understand the importance of medication compliance (anxiolytics, antidepressants, sleep inducers), working with any cognitive therapy prescribed, and the benefit of stopping the use of caffeine or other stimulants.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluoxetine -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: The 5-HT2C antagonism is thought to contribute to the anxiety, insomnia, and agitation patients perceive with fluoxetine. Patients may even have a panic attack with the administration of fluoxetine. Thus, the clinician's responsibility is to educate patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Anxiety (Nursing) -- Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: SNRIs (venlafaxine and duloxetine) are considered as effective as SSRIs and also are considered first-line treatment, particularly for generalized anxiety disorder (GAD).

retrieved_docs.title: Late-Life Depression -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: These include bupropion and mirtazapine. Mirtazapine is also a second-line agent. Besides antidepressant action, it is also useful in patients with insomnia, anorexia, and restlessness. When comparing mirtazapine with paroxetine, the tolerability profile was similar. Common side effects include sedation, increased appetite causing weight gain, dry mouth, and constipation. Noradrenergic effects are more pronounced over the anti-histaminergic effects at higher dosages and thus lower sedative effects. In patients with symptoms of lethargy or fatigue, bupropion is thought to be an activating agent. Bupropion tends to lower the seizure threshold and is contraindicated in patients with a seizure disorder, concurrent use with CNS depressants, alcohol detoxification, and diagnosis of bulimia nervosa. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2289
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder caused by drug is classified as اضطراب القلق بسبب حالة طبية عامة, meaning it is a type of anxiety disorder that arises secondary to a general medical condition, specifically the pharmacologic effects of a medication. This relationship highlights that drug‑induced anxiety is considered within the broader category of anxiety disorders linked to underlying medical illnesses, emphasizing the importance of recognizing medication side effects as a distinct etiological factor in patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2702
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is recognized as an appropriate descriptor for pharmacotherapy (farmakoterapie) in the context of anxiety (القلق), fatigue (التَّعَب), and insomnia (الاِختِنَاق). Consequently, clinicians may consider these conditions—anxiety, fatigue, and insomnia—as permissible qualifiers when selecting or adjusting pharmacologic treatments. Moreover, fatigue (التَّعَب) is identified as having a defining characteristic of anxiety (القلق), indicating that fatigue commonly manifests as a core feature of anxiety disorders. This interconnected understanding helps guide comprehensive patient assessment and informs the choice of pharmacotherapy by acknowledging that anxiety, fatigue, and insomnia often coexist and influence treatment decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 390
retrieved_docs.content: Bupropion hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride are both contraindicated for use in patients experiencing withdrawal syndrome (متلازمة الانسحاب), indicating that these antidepressant formulations should be avoided in this clinical context. In contrast, tofisopam may be employed as a therapeutic option to treat withdrawal syndrome, suggesting a potential role for this anxiolytic agent in managing the symptoms associated with substance discontinuation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2928
retrieved_docs.content: Citalopram, a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor used to treat depression and anxiety disorders, may have its therapeutic outcomes influenced by تعاطي التَبغ (tobacco use or smoking). Smoking can alter the metabolism of citalopram, potentially affecting its efficacy and side‑effect profile. Therefore, clinicians should assess a patient’s smoking status when initiating or adjusting citalopram therapy to ensure optimal treatment response and safety.

Pharmacotherapy is recognized as an appropriate and permitted treatment option for both anxiety and nonspecific chest pain, highlighting the relevance of medication management in addressing these interrelated conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 123
retrieved_docs.content: recognized, with specific forms such as psychoactive‑substance withdrawal, SSRI withdrawal, and selective‑serotonin‑reuptake‑inhibitor withdrawal. In patients experiencing such withdrawal, bupropion (including its salt forms) is contraindicated, whereas tofisopam may be employed to treat withdrawal symptoms. Overall, the knowledge network connects antidepressants to their parent psychoanaleptic and psychotropic categories, enumerates their diverse members and ancillary uses (e.g., smoking cessation, ADHD treatment), links them to drug‑induced adverse reactions, and delineates the overlapping yet distinct roles of psychostimulants, centrally acting sympathomimetics, and nootropics within the same pharmacologic landscape, highlighting the importance of nuanced prescribing and patient‑management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1547
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyzine hydrochloride and tofisopam are identified as therapeutic options that may be used to treat متلازمة الانسحاب (withdrawal syndrome), offering clinicians potential pharmacologic strategies for managing the symptoms of this condition. In contrast, both bupropion hydrobromide and bupropion hydrochloride are contraindicated in patients with متلازمة الانسحاب, indicating that these formulations of bupropion should be avoided in the presence of withdrawal syndrome to prevent adverse effects. This distinction guides appropriate drug selection, promoting the use of hydroxyzine hydrochloride or tofisopam for treatment while excluding bupropion products in affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1634
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition is classified as a specific type of anxiety disorders and is defined by particular diagnostic criteria that guide its identification and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1262
retrieved_docs.content: Bupropion, including its hydrobromide and hydrochloride salt forms, is contraindicated in patients who are experiencing متلازمة الانسحاب (withdrawal syndrome), indicating that this antidepressant and smoking‑cessation medication should be avoided during the acute phase of substance withdrawal. In contrast, tofisopam may be employed as a therapeutic option to treat متلازمة الانسحاب, suggesting a potential role for this anxiolytic agent in managing withdrawal‑related symptoms. Recognizing the contraindication of bupropion and the possible benefit of tofisopam is important for clinicians when selecting safe and effective pharmacologic strategies for individuals undergoing withdrawal.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2292
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition, also described as anxiety disorder due to a known physiological condition, is a distinct psychiatric diagnosis that requires the presence of specific diagnostic criteria. This disorder is characterized by anxiety symptoms that are directly attributable to an underlying medical or physiological condition, and clinicians must identify the causal medical condition and apply the established criteria to confirm the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2452
retrieved_docs.content: Gabapentin, an anticonvulsant and neuropathic pain medication, may be employed in the therapeutic management of رُهاب (phobia). Although primarily indicated for seizures and neuropathic pain, gabapentin is sometimes used off‑label to alleviate anxiety‑related symptoms associated with specific phobic disorders, providing clinicians with an additional pharmacologic option for patients who experience heightened fear responses. This potential application underscores the broader utility of gabapentin in addressing certain anxiety conditions beyond its conventional uses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 89
retrieved_docs.content: desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetine hydrochloride and trazodone because of the risk of serotonin‑related adverse events. Tricyclic antidepressants are contraindicated when co‑administered with dronedarone hydrochloride. Withdrawal syndrome associated with SSRIs results from the SSRIs themselves; bupropion (including its hydrochloride and hydrobromide salts) is contraindicated in patients experiencing this syndrome, whereas tofisofam may be employed for its treatment. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the multidimensional classification of many drugs—anticonvulsants, tranquilizers, antidepressants, psychostimulants, nootropics and smoking‑cessation agents—and underscore the clinical importance of recognizing overlapping therapeutic categories, structural linkages, and contraindications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2212
retrieved_docs.content: Citalopram is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor prescribed for depressive and anxiety disorders. Alcohol dependence (الاعتماد على الكحول) is a substance‑use disorder that can interfere with the effectiveness and safety of citalopram, rather than serve as a treatment for it. Clinicians should be aware that patients with alcohol dependence may require careful monitoring, dose adjustments, or alternative therapeutic strategies when using citalopram.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1742
retrieved_docs.content: Citalopram, desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetine hydrochloride, and trazodone are all contraindicated for co‑administration with linezolid. Linezolid is a protein synthesis inhibitor antibiotic that also exhibits reversible monoamine‑oxidase inhibition. Because of this pharmacologic profile, concurrent use of linezolid with serotonergic agents such as citalopram, desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetine hydrochloride, or the atypical antidepressant trazodone can precipitate serotonin syndrome. Recognizing these contraindications is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care.
